claude-1-1-agentic-loop./objectiveTitle: Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./title: Agentic loops: enabling autonomous action while ensuring the agent knows when to stop
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./summary: A customer support assistant must look up orders, try tools, and stop on its own once it has sufficient evidence. This lesson connects observation, action selection, tool calls, and stopping conditions into a verifiable decision.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./opening/0: A customer support assistant must look up orders, try tools, and stop on its own once it has sufficient evidence.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./opening/1: Do not study objective 1.1 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present a nearly correct system and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/0/title: A mental model from input to outcome
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Observation is the starting point, action selection determines how the system changes state, and tool calls and stopping conditions provide the evidence needed to determine whether the outcome is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem as this sequence rather than choosing a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Repeat the cycle of receiving state → selecting an action → executing → evaluating; set limits on iterations, tokens, and time.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before choosing a mechanism.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stopping conditions.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/1/title: Choosing an architecture and evaluating trade-offs
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Use a loop only when the execution path depends on intermediate results; fixed flows should use deterministic workflows.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good solution does more than work on the happy path. It also clarifies authority, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare all options using the same criteria.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A loop without convergence criteria will repeat the same tool call or exhaust its budget.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to represent assumptions as state or structured data, establish explicit limits, and preserve enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “absent,” “not found yet,” and “cannot be checked.”
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not conceal errors with empty results.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./diagram/alt: Agentic Architecture & Orchestration diagram for objective 1.1
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./diagram/caption: Read from left to right: a request passes through control boundaries, actions, and a feedback loop before becoming an accepted outcome.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./visuals/0/caption: Stop when the ticket has a status, a cause, and a next action; escalate to a human if data is still missing after three lookup attempts.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./visuals/0/nodes/1/responsibility: Narrow down the next set of options
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./visuals/0/nodes/2/responsibility: Narrow down the next set of options
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./visuals/0/nodes/3/responsibility: Produce evidence and decide whether to stop
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Choosing an implementation approach for scenario 1.1
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/requirement: A customer support assistant must look up orders, try tools, and stop on its own once it has sufficient evidence.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/0: Do not expand permissions or data access beyond what is necessary.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/1: Errors, costs, and completion status must be observable.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/2: The outcome must be independently verifiable.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/0/fit: Appropriate when intermediate results determine the next step and the system must select an action.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/0/limita: Consumes more context and requires explicit stopping conditions.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/name: Deterministic workflow with a validation gate
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/fit: Appropriate when the sequence, permissions, and stopping conditions are known in advance.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/limita: Less flexible when handling new situations that have not been modeled.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Use a loop only when the execution path depends on intermediate results; fixed flows should use deterministic workflows.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Define the state and side effects.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Assess the risk of a tool call failure.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Choose the option that produces evidence at an acceptable cost.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./comparison/0/useWhen: The execution path depends on new observations and has multiple valid branches.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./comparison/0/tradeOff: Requires a budget, guardrails, and better observability.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./comparison/1/useWhen: The sequence and state-transition conditions are clear, or compliance requirements apply.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./comparison/1/tradeOff: Less adaptable, but easier to audit and test.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Simply adding a longer prompt can fix a loop that lacks convergence criteria and will repeat the same tool call or exhaust its budget.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./commonMistakes/0/better: Encode critical conditions in the schema, state, permissions, or validation instead of relying only on wording.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./commonMistakes/0/reason: Architectural controls must exist at execution boundaries, not only in the intended behavior.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Treating a plausible-sounding answer as proof of success.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./commonMistakes/1/better: Require evidence tied to the stopping condition and verify the actual side effects.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./commonMistakes/1/reason: Fluency does not prove that the system completed the task correctly.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./examFocus/0: Recognize when observation must be separated from action selection.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./examFocus/1: Choose the option that reduces the risk of this failure mode: a loop without convergence criteria will repeat the same tool call or exhaust its budget.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./examFocus/2: Explain the trade-off instead of merely naming a Claude feature.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/context: A customer support assistant must look up orders, try tools, and stop on its own once it has sufficient evidence.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/learnerRole: You are the architect responsible for boundaries, reliability, and operating costs.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/tasks/0: Diagram the states from observation to the stopping condition.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/tasks/1: Choose either a flexible agent or a deterministic workflow and provide two reasons.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/tasks/2: Design a failure path for the following case: a loop without convergence criteria will repeat the same tool call or exhaust its budget.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Identify the minimum required data and permissions.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/reasoningSteps/1: List success, retryable error, and terminal error states.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Tie each conclusion to verifiable evidence.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/deliverables/1: A short decision record containing the chosen option and its trade-offs.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: Stop when the ticket has a status, a cause, and a next action; escalate to a human if data is still missing after three lookup attempts.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: Repeat the cycle of receiving state → selecting an action → executing → evaluating; set limits on iterations, tokens, and time.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: Acceptance criterion: the stopping condition must produce evidence, and the system must stop within its budget.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: What changes if tool calls are unreliable?
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: Which metrics would you use to measure quality and cost?
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./recap/0: Repeat the cycle of receiving state → selecting an action → executing → evaluating; set limits on iterations, tokens, and time.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./recap/1: Use a loop only when the execution path depends on intermediate results; fixed flows should use deterministic workflows.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./recap/2: Failure mode to remember: a loop without convergence criteria will repeat the same tool call or exhaust its budget.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./selfCheck/0: Explain observation and stopping conditions to a beginner.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./selfCheck/1: When would you choose a workflow instead of an agent loop?
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./selfCheck/2: How would you verify this example: stop when the ticket has a status, a cause, and a next action; escalate to a human if data is still missing after three lookup attempts?

claude-1-1-agentic-loop./vocabulary/3/meaning: An outcome signal used to confirm completion or stop execution.
claude-1-1-agentic-loop./vocabulary/3/examHint: In a scenario, look for signs that a stopping condition is missing, placed at the wrong boundary, or not verified.

claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./objectiveTitle: Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./title: Coordinator and subagent: dividing roles for parallel processing
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./summary: A research report requires three separate branches for sourcing, verification, and synthesis without mixing responsibilities. This lesson connects the coordinator, specialized subagents, fan-out/fan-in, and result consolidation to produce a verifiable decision.
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./opening/0: A research report requires three separate branches for sourcing, verification, and synthesis without mixing responsibilities.
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./opening/1: Do not learn objective 1.2 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present an approximately correct system and ask you to select the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./sections/0/title: A mental model from input to outcome
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./sections/0/paragraphs/0: The coordinator is the starting point, specialized subagents determine how the system changes state, and fan-out/fan-in and result consolidation provide evidence for assessing whether the outcome is trustworthy. Always interpret the problem as this sequence instead of selecting a feature merely because its name is familiar.
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./sections/0/paragraphs/1: The coordinator decomposes the work, assigns clear output contracts, runs tasks independently when possible, and then synthesizes the results using shared criteria.
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before selecting a mechanism.
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stopping conditions.
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./sections/1/title: How to select an architecture and evaluate trade-offs

claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach must do more than work on the happy path. It must also clarify authority, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare all options using the same criteria.
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Ambiguous task assignments cause multiple agents to duplicate work or return outputs in formats that cannot be combined.
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to represent assumptions as state or structured data, establish clear limits, and preserve enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “none exists,” “not yet found,” and “cannot be verified.”
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not mask errors with empty results.
claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.

claude-1-2-coordinator-subagents./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/limita: Less flexible when handling new situations that have not been modeled.

claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./objectiveTitle: Implement multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff patterns
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./title: Multi-step workflows, validation gates, and handoffs
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./shortTitle: Multi-step workflows, validation gates, and handoffs
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./summary: A production change must undergo analysis, testing, and approval before deployment. This lesson connects a state machine, validation gate, handoff contract, and compensation to form a verifiable decision.
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./opening/0: A production change must undergo analysis, testing, and approval before deployment.
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./opening/1: Do not study objective 1.4 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present a nearly correct system and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/0/title: A mental model from input to outcome
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/0/paragraphs/0: The state machine is the starting point, the validation gate determines how the system transitions between states, and the handoff contract and compensation provide evidence for determining whether the outcome is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem in this sequence instead of selecting a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Each step declares its input, output, entry and exit conditions, and recovery method; the state transitions only when the evidence is valid.
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before selecting a mechanism.
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stop conditions.
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/1/title: How to choose an architecture and evaluate trade-offs
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Use an enforced workflow for processes with compliance requirements or a mandatory sequence; use an autonomous agent for low-risk exploration.
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good solution does more than work on the happy path. It also clarifies permissions, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare the options using the same criteria.
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/1/takeaways/1: Favor boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Merely listing the steps in the prompt does not ensure that the agent will follow the sequence or maintain consistent state.
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to convert assumptions into state or structured data, set clear limits, and retain enough provenance for the next step to distinguish among “absent,” “not yet found,” and “cannot be verified.”
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not mask errors with empty results.
claude-1-4-enforced-workflows./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.

claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./objectiveTitle: Apply Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception and data normalization
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./title: Hooks: Intercepting Tool Calls and Normalizing Data
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./summary: Different tools return dates, times, and errors in different formats, but the audit system requires a consistent format. This lesson connects pre-hooks, post-hooks, normalization, and the audit trail into a verifiable decision.
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./opening/0: Different tools return dates, times, and errors in different formats, but the audit system requires a consistent format.
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./opening/1: Do not study objective 1.5 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present a nearly correct system and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./sections/0/title: A Mental Model from Input to Outcome
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./sections/0/paragraphs/0: The pre-hook is the starting point, the post-hook determines how the system transitions between states, and normalization and the audit trail provide evidence for assessing whether the result is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem as this sequence rather than selecting a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./sections/0/paragraphs/1: The pre-hook validates and masks sensitive data; the post-hook normalizes results, measures latency, and attaches a correlation ID.
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before selecting a mechanism.
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and termination conditions.

claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Place cross-cutting logic in hooks; keep business logic within the tool to prevent hooks from becoming opaque bottlenecks.
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good solution does more than work on the happy path. It also clarifies permissions, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare the options using the same criteria.
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A hook that silently modifies content without logging it destroys provenance and makes debugging extremely difficult.
claude-1-5-sdk-hooks./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to represent assumptions as states or structured data, set clear limits, and preserve enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “absent,” “not yet found,” and “unable to verify.”

claude-1-6-task-decomposition./objectiveTitle: Design task decomposition strategies for complex workflows
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./title: Decomposing Tasks by Dependencies and Boundaries
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./shortTitle: Decomposing Tasks by Dependencies and Boundaries
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./summary: A migration includes schema analysis, data transfer, testing, and a rollback plan. This lesson connects the dependency graph, task granularity, parallelization, and completion criteria into a verifiable decision.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./opening/0: A migration includes schema analysis, data transfer, testing, and a rollback plan.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./opening/1: Do not learn objective 1.6 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present a nearly correct system and then ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/0/title: A Mental Model from Input to Outcome
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/0/paragraphs/0: The dependency graph is the starting point, task granularity determines how the system transitions between states, and parallelization and completion criteria provide evidence that the outcome is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem in this sequence instead of choosing a feature merely because its name is familiar.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Decompose the work by verifiable deliverables, identify dependencies, and parallelize only branches that do not share writable state.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before choosing a mechanism.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stopping conditions.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/1/title: Choosing an Architecture and Evaluating Trade-offs
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Tasks that are too small increase overhead; tasks that are too large make it difficult for the agent to self-check and retry selectively.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach does more than work on the happy path. It also clarifies permissions, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare approaches using the same criteria.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Dividing work into equally sized parts instead of following dependencies creates race conditions and handoffs with insufficient data.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The remedy is to represent assumptions as explicit state or structured data, set clear limits, and retain enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “none exists,” “not yet found,” and “cannot be checked.”
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not conceal errors with empty results.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./diagram/alt: Agentic Architecture & Orchestration diagram for objective 1.6
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./diagram/caption: Read from left to right: the request passes through control boundaries, actions, and a feedback loop before becoming an accepted outcome.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./visuals/0/caption: Schema analysis and test fixture creation run in parallel; the migration begins only after the schema mapping is approved.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./visuals/0/nodes/1/responsibility: Narrow the next set of options
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./visuals/0/nodes/2/responsibility: Narrow the next set of options
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./visuals/0/nodes/3/responsibility: Produce evidence and determine when to stop
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./decisionWalkthroughs/0/requirement: A migration includes schema analysis, data transfer, testing, and a rollback plan.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/0: Do not expand permissions or data access beyond what is necessary.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/1: Errors, costs, and completion status must be observable.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/2: The outcome must be independently verifiable.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/0/fit: Appropriate when intermediate results determine the next step and task granularity must be adjusted.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/0/limita: Consumes more context and requires clear stopping conditions.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/fit: Appropriate when the sequence, permissions, and completion criteria are known in advance.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/limita: Less flexible when handling new situations that have not been modeled.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Tasks that are too small increase overhead; tasks that are too large make it difficult for the agent to self-check and retry selectively.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Bound the state and side effects.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Assess the risk if parallel execution fails.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Choose the approach that produces evidence at an acceptable cost.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./comparison/0/useWhen: The path depends on new observations and multiple branches are valid.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./comparison/0/tradeOff: Requires stronger budgets, guardrails, and observability.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./comparison/1/useWhen: The sequence and state-transition conditions are clear, or compliance requirements apply.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./comparison/1/tradeOff: Less adaptable but easier to audit and test.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Simply using a longer prompt can solve the race conditions and incomplete handoffs caused by dividing work into equally sized parts instead of following dependencies.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./commonMistakes/0/better: Encode critical conditions in the schema, state, permissions, or validation rather than relying solely on wording.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./commonMistakes/0/reason: Architectural controls must exist at execution boundaries, not merely as intentions.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Judging success by whether an answer sounds plausible.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./commonMistakes/1/better: Require evidence tied to the completion criteria and verify actual side effects.
claude-1-6-task-decomposition./commonMistakes/1/reason: Fluency does not prove that the system completed the task correctly.

claude-1-6-task-decomposition./examFocus/2: Explain the trade-off instead of merely naming a Claude feature.

claude-1-6-task-decomposition./recap/1: Tasks that are too small increase overhead; tasks that are too large make it difficult for the agent to self-check and retry selectively.

claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./title: Safe Sessions, Checkpoints, Resumption, and Forking
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./shortTitle: Safe Sessions, Checkpoints, Resumption, and Forking
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./summary: A long-running task is interrupted and must resume without rerunning steps that have already written data. This lesson connects session state, checkpoints, idempotency, and fork isolation into a verifiable decision.
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./opening/0: A long-running task is interrupted and must resume without rerunning steps that have already written data.
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./opening/1: Do not study objective 1.7 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present a nearly correct system and ask you to select the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/0/title: A Mental Model from Input to Outcome
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Session state is the starting point, checkpoints determine how the system transitions between states, and idempotency and fork isolation provide evidence that the outcome is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem in this sequence instead of selecting a feature merely because its name is familiar.
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Persist state after each significant milestone, including the input hash, result, and side effects; resume from the first incomplete step.
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the initial state before selecting a mechanism.
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have explicit success, failure, and stopping conditions.
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/1/title: Choosing an Architecture and Evaluating Trade-offs
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Fork from the same checkpoint to test an alternative approach, but do not share a write area unless a locking mechanism is in place.
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A sound approach must do more than work on the happy path. It must also clarify permissions, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare all options using the same criteria.
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and roll back.
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Saving only the conversation without recording side effects can cause a resumed task to repeat a payment or create a duplicate issue.
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to turn assumptions into state or structured data, establish clear limits, and preserve enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “absent,” “not yet found,” and “cannot be checked.”
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not mask errors with empty results.
claude-1-7-sessions-resume-fork./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.

claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./objectiveTitle: Design effective tool interfaces with clear descriptions and boundaries
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./title: Design Tools That Are Easy to Select and Hard to Misuse
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./shortTitle: Design Tools That Are Easy to Select and Hard to Misuse
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./summary: The agent has several similar search tools and often selects the wrong data source. This lesson connects single responsibility, meaningful names, JSON Schema, and side-effect boundaries into a verifiable decision.
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./opening/0: The agent has several similar search tools and often selects the wrong data source.
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./opening/1: Do not learn objective 2.1 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present a nearly correct system and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./sections/0/title: A Mental Model from Input to Outcome
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Single responsibility is the starting point, meaningful names determine how the system transitions between states, and JSON Schema and side-effect boundaries provide evidence for determining whether the outcome is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem as this sequence instead of selecting a feature merely because its name is familiar.
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Describe a tool in terms of when to use it, when not to use it, its required inputs, and its output; make the schema as strict as possible.
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the initial state before choosing a mechanism.
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stopping conditions.
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./sections/1/title: Choosing an Architecture and Evaluating Trade-offs
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Separate tools when their permissions, side effects, or failure modes differ; combine them when they are always used within the same transaction.
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach does more than work on the happy path. It also clarifies permissions, context cost, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare all options using the same criteria.
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Generic tool names such as execute or manage, combined with unconstrained parameters, force the model to infer intent and can produce incorrect payloads.
claude-2-1-tool-interfaces./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to turn assumptions into explicit state or structured data, set clear limits, and retain enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “none,” “not found yet,” and “unable to verify.”

claude-2-4-mcp-integration./objectiveTitle: Integrate MCP servers into Claude Code and agent workflows
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./title: Connect an MCP server to Claude Code and an agent
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./shortTitle: Connect an MCP server to Claude Code and an agent
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./summary: The development team wants Claude to access the issue tracker and internal database through a standard interface. This lesson connects the MCP host/client/server model, stdio and HTTP, tools/resources/prompts, and the trust boundary into a verifiable decision.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./opening/0: The development team wants Claude to access the issue tracker and internal database through a standard interface.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./opening/1: Do not study section 2.4 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present an almost-correct system and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/0/title: A mental model from input to outcome
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/0/paragraphs/0: The MCP host/client/server model is the starting point, stdio and HTTP determine how the system transitions between states, and tools/resources/prompts and the trust boundary provide the evidence needed to determine whether the outcome is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem in this sequence instead of choosing a feature merely because its name is familiar.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Choose the transport based on the environment, declare configuration at the appropriate scope, verify capabilities, and manage credentials outside the repo.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before choosing a mechanism.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stop conditions.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/1/title: How to choose an architecture and assess trade-offs
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/1/paragraphs/0: stdio is suitable for a local process; HTTP is suitable for shared services that require auth, observability, and lifecycle management.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach does more than work on the happy path. It also clarifies permissions, context cost, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare the options using the same criteria.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Trusting every MCP server or committing a token to configuration expands the trust boundary without control.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The fix is to turn assumptions into explicit states or structured data, set clear limits, and retain enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “absent,” “not found yet,” and “unable to verify.”
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not hide errors behind empty results.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./diagram/alt: Tool Design & MCP Integration diagram for section 2.4
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./diagram/caption: Read from left to right: the request passes through the control boundary, action, and feedback loop before becoming an accepted outcome.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./visuals/0/caption: Project config contains only the command/URL; the token comes from a secret store, and the tool records the approval request.

claude-2-4-mcp-integration./visuals/0/nodes/3/responsibility: Generate evidence and determine when to stop
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Choose an implementation approach for scenario 2.4
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/requirement: The development team wants Claude to access the issue tracker and internal database through a standard interface.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/0: Do not expand permissions or data access beyond the necessary scope.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/1: Errors, costs, and completion status must be observable.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/2: The outcome must be independently verifiable.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/0/name: A flexible mechanism orchestrated by the agent
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/0/fit: Suitable when intermediate results determine the next step and both stdio and HTTP are needed.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/0/limita: Consumes more context and requires clear stop conditions.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/name: Deterministic workflow with a validation gate
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/fit: Suitable when the sequence, permissions, and trust boundary are known in advance.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/limita: Less flexible when handling new situations that have not been modeled.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: stdio is suitable for a local process; HTTP is suitable for shared services that require auth, observability, and lifecycle management.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Define the state and side effects.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Assess the risk if tools/resources/prompts fail.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Choose the approach that produces evidence at an acceptable cost.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./comparison/0/useWhen: The path depends on new observations and multiple branches are valid.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./comparison/0/tradeOff: Requires a budget, guardrails, and better observability.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./comparison/1/useWhen: The sequence and state-transition conditions are clear, or compliance requirements apply.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./comparison/1/tradeOff: Less adaptable, but easier to audit and test.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Simply adding a longer prompt can address the uncontrolled expansion of the trust boundary caused by trusting every mcp server or committing a token to configuration.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./commonMistakes/0/better: Encode critical conditions in the schema, state, permissions, or validation instead of relying solely on wording.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./commonMistakes/0/reason: Architectural controls must exist at the execution boundary, not only as intentions.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Judging success by whether the response sounds plausible.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./commonMistakes/1/better: Require evidence tied to the trust boundary and verify the actual side effects.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./commonMistakes/1/reason: Fluency does not prove that the system completed the task correctly.

claude-2-4-mcp-integration./examFocus/2: Explain the trade-off instead of merely naming a Claude feature.

claude-2-4-mcp-integration./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: Choose the transport based on the environment, declare configuration at the appropriate scope, verify capabilities, and manage credentials outside the repo.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: Acceptance criterion: the trust boundary must produce evidence, and the system must stop within budget.
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: What changes if tools/resources/prompts are untrusted?
claude-2-4-mcp-integration./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: Which metrics would you use to measure quality and cost?

claude-2-4-mcp-integration./recap/1: stdio is suitable for a local process; HTTP is suitable for shared services that require auth, observability, and lifecycle management.

claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./summary: The monorepo has shared rules and separate conventions for the backend, mobile, and documentation areas. This lesson connects hierarchy, scope, specificity, and maintainability into a verifiable decision.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./opening/0: The monorepo has shared rules and separate conventions for the backend, mobile, and documentation areas.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./opening/1: Do not learn section 3.1 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present a nearly correct system and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/0/title: A Mental Model from Input to Outcome
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Hierarchy is the starting point, scope determines how the system transitions between states, and specificity and maintainability provide evidence that the outcome is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem in this sequence instead of choosing a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Place shared principles at the root and subtree-specific rules near the code; write verifiable instructions and avoid one-off troubleshooting history.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the initial state before choosing a mechanism.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must define success, failure, and stopping conditions.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/1/title: Choosing an Architecture and Evaluating Trade-offs
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Move a rule closer to the files when it applies only to one area; keep the root concise so it does not dilute every task.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach must do more than work on the happy path. It must also clarify permissions, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare options using the same criteria.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Putting every exception in the root bloats the context and creates unpredictable conflicts.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The remedy is to turn assumptions into explicit state or structured data, establish clear limits, and retain enough provenance for later steps to distinguish among “absent,” “not yet found,” and “unable to verify.”
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not hide errors behind empty results.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./diagram/alt: Claude Code Configuration & Workflows diagram for section 3.1
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./diagram/caption: Read from left to right: the request passes through control boundaries, actions, and a feedback loop before becoming an accepted outcome.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./visuals/0/caption: The root defines the test policy; backend/CLAUDE.md defines migration and API conventions.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./visuals/0/nodes/3/responsibility: Produce evidence and determine when to stop
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Choosing an Implementation Approach for Scenario 3.1
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/requirement: The monorepo has shared rules and separate conventions for the backend, mobile, and documentation areas.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/0: Do not expand permissions or data access beyond the necessary scope.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/1: Errors, costs, and completion status must be observable.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/2: The outcome must be independently verifiable.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/0/fit: Suitable when intermediate results determine the next step and scoping is required.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/0/limita: Consumes more context and requires clear stopping conditions.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/name: Deterministic Workflow with a Validation Gate
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/fit: Suitable when the sequence, permissions, and maintainability requirements are known in advance.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/limita: Less flexible when handling new situations that have not been modeled.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Move a rule closer to the files when it applies only to one area; keep the root concise so it does not dilute every task.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Bound the state and side effects.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Assess the risk if specificity fails.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Choose the option that produces evidence at an acceptable cost.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./comparison/0/useWhen: The path depends on new observations and has multiple valid branches.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./comparison/0/tradeOff: Requires a budget, guardrails, and better observability.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./comparison/1/useWhen: The sequence and state-transition conditions are clear, or compliance requirements apply.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./comparison/1/tradeOff: Less adaptable but easier to audit and test.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Assuming that a longer prompt alone can solve the problem of putting every exception in the root, which bloats the context and creates unpredictable conflicts.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./commonMistakes/0/better: Encode important conditions in schemas, state, permissions, or validation instead of relying solely on wording.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./commonMistakes/0/reason: Architectural controls must exist at execution boundaries, not merely as intentions.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Judging success by whether the response sounds plausible.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./commonMistakes/1/better: Require evidence tied to maintainability and verify actual side effects.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./commonMistakes/1/reason: Fluency does not prove that the system completed the task correctly.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./examFocus/0: Recognize when hierarchy must be separated from scope.
claude-3-1-claude-md-hierarchy./examFocus/1: Choose the option that reduces the risk of this failure mode: putting every exception in the root bloats the context and creates unpredictable conflicts.

claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./title: Path-specific rules: load conventions only when needed
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./summary: SQL migration rules should not affect tasks that only modify the UI. This lesson connects path matchers, conditional context, local conventions, and conflict resolution into a verifiable decision.
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./opening/0: SQL migration rules should not affect tasks that only modify the UI.
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./opening/1: Do not study section 3.3 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present a nearly correct system and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/0/title: A mental model from input to outcome
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/0/paragraphs/0: The path matcher is the starting point, conditional context determines how the system transitions between states, and local conventions and conflict resolution provide evidence that the outcome is reliable. Always analyze the problem in this sequence instead of choosing a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Attach each rule to a specific path pattern, keep its content self-contained, and verify that the more local rule takes precedence as expected.
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before selecting a mechanism.
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must define success, failure, and stopping conditions.
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/1/title: Choosing an architecture and evaluating trade-offs
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Use path rules for technical standards with a verifiable scope, not as a repository for general knowledge.
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good solution does more than work on the happy path. It also clarifies permissions, context cost, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare all options using the same criteria.
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/2/paragraphs/0: An overly broad pattern causes the rule to load every time; an overly narrow pattern misses generated files or related tests.
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The remedy is to represent assumptions as states or structured data, set explicit limits, and preserve enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “absent,” “not found yet,” and “unable to verify.”
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not mask errors with empty results.
claude-3-3-path-specific-rules./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.

claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./objectiveTitle: Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./title: When to Plan and When to Execute Directly
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./shortTitle: When to Plan and When to Execute Directly
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./summary: A minor rename and a payment migration have very different levels of uncertainty and blast radius. This lesson connects uncertainty, blast radius, reversibility, and approval into a verifiable decision.
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./opening/0: A minor rename and a payment migration have very different levels of uncertainty and blast radius.
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./opening/1: Do not learn section 3.4 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present an approximate system and then ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/0/title: A Mental Model from Input to Outcome
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/0/paragraphs/0: uncertainty is the starting point, blast radius determines how the system transitions between states, and reversibility and approval provide evidence that the outcome is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem in this sequence instead of choosing a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Assess requirement clarity, scope of impact, reversibility, and the cost of errors; use plan mode when exploration or a firm decision is needed.
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the initial state before choosing a mechanism.
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have conditions for success, failure, and termination.
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/1/title: Choosing an Architecture and Evaluating Trade-offs
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Execute directly for small, reversible changes with clear tests; use plan mode for cross-cutting changes, production data, or multiple architectural options.
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach must do more than work on the happy path. It must also clarify permissions, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare all options using the same criteria.
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Planning every task causes delays; skipping planning for high-risk changes causes constraints to be discovered too late.
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to represent assumptions as state or structured data, set clear limits, and retain enough provenance for later steps to distinguish between “absent,” “not yet found,” and “unable to verify.”
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not conceal errors with empty results.
claude-3-4-plan-vs-execution./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.

claude-3-5-iterative-refinement./title: Iterative refinement: every iteration must produce a better signal
claude-3-5-iterative-refinement./summary: An extractor achieves 82% accuracy and needs to improve without breaking cases it already handles correctly. This lesson connects the baseline, feedback signal, small delta, and regression check into a verifiable decision.
claude-3-5-iterative-refinement./opening/0: An extractor achieves 82% accuracy and needs to improve without breaking cases it already handles correctly.
claude-3-5-iterative-refinement./opening/1: Do not learn section 3.5 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present a system that is nearly correct and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-3-5-iterative-refinement./sections/0/title: A mental model from input to outcome
claude-3-5-iterative-refinement./sections/0/paragraphs/0: The baseline is the starting point, the feedback signal determines how the system changes state, and the small delta and regression check provide evidence that the result is reliable. Always analyze the problem as this sequence instead of choosing a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-3-5-iterative-refinement./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Define the metric, retain failure cases, change one factor per iteration, and run the evaluator before accepting the result.

claude-3-5-iterative-refinement./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are observable and easy to roll back.

claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./objectiveTitle: Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./title: Claude Code in CI/CD with Least Privilege
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./shortTitle: Claude Code in CI/CD with Least Privilege
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./summary: The pipeline needs automated PR reviews but must not merge changes on its own or read unrelated secrets. This lesson connects non-interactive mode, scoped tokens, deterministic gates, and artifacts to produce a verifiable decision.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./opening/0: The pipeline needs automated PR reviews but must not merge changes on its own or read unrelated secrets.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./opening/1: Do not learn objective 3.6 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present an almost-correct system and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/0/title: A Mental Model from Input to Outcome
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/0/paragraphs/0: non-interactive mode is the starting point, a scoped token determines how the system changes state, and a deterministic gate and artifact provide evidence for assessing whether the outcome is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem as this sequence instead of choosing a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Pin permissions, restrict tools, pass the diff explicitly, set a timeout, and save the report as an artifact; fail safely when the model/API fails.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before choosing a mechanism.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have explicit success, failure, and stop conditions.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/1/title: Choosing an Architecture and Evaluating Trade-offs
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Let Claude produce analyses or recommendations; keep build, test, and deterministic policy gates responsible for pass/fail decisions.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach must work beyond the happy path. It must also clarify permissions, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare all options using the same criteria.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Giving an agent broad write access in CI can turn untrusted PR input into dangerous actions.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to represent assumptions as explicit state or structured data, set clear limits, and retain enough provenance for downstream steps to distinguish among “absent,” “not yet found,” and “unable to verify.”
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not conceal errors with empty results.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.

claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/0: Do not expand permissions or data access beyond what is necessary.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/1: Errors, costs, and completion status must be observable.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/2: The outcome must be independently verifiable.

claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/0/limita: Consumes more context and requires explicit stop conditions.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/name: Deterministic Workflow with a Validation Gate
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/fit: Suitable when the sequence, permissions, and artifact are known in advance.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/limita: Less flexible when handling new situations that have not been modeled.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Let Claude produce analyses or recommendations; keep build, test, and deterministic policy gates responsible for pass/fail decisions.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Bound the state and side effects.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Assess the risk if the deterministic gate fails.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Choose an option that produces evidence at an acceptable cost.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./comparison/0/useWhen: The path depends on new observations and has multiple valid branches.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./comparison/0/tradeOff: Requires a budget, guardrails, and better observability.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./comparison/1/useWhen: The sequence and state-transition conditions are clear, or compliance requirements apply.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./comparison/1/tradeOff: Less adaptive, but easier to audit and test.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Simply adding a longer prompt will address the risk that giving an agent broad write access in CI can turn untrusted PR input into dangerous actions.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./commonMistakes/0/better: Encode critical conditions in the schema, state, permissions, or validation instead of relying only on wording.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./commonMistakes/0/reason: Architectural controls must exist at the execution boundary, not merely in the stated intent.
claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Judging success by whether the response sounds plausible.

claude-3-6-claude-code-cicd./commonMistakes/1/reason: Fluency does not prove that the system completed the task correctly.

claude-4-1-explicit-criteria./summary: Two people may interpret a “good summary” differently, resulting in inconsistent output. This lesson connects the role and goal, input boundary, acceptance criteria, and output contract into a verifiable decision.

Always analyze the problem in this sequence instead of selecting a feature merely because its name is familiar.

claude-4-2-few-shot./summary: The classifier often confuses “refund request” with “billing question” in edge cases. This lesson connects representative example, edge case, label balance, and format consistency into a verifiable decision.
claude-4-2-few-shot./opening/0: The classifier often confuses “refund request” with “billing question” in edge cases.
claude-4-2-few-shot./opening/1: Do not learn section 4.2 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present a nearly correct system and then ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery method.
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/0/title: A mental model from input to outcome
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/0/paragraphs/0: A representative example is the starting point, an edge case determines how the system transitions between states, and label balance and format consistency provide evidence of whether the result is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem as this sequence instead of choosing a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Choose a small number of examples that cover the decision boundaries, keep the format consistent, and ensure that the examples do not conflict with the criteria.
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before choosing a mechanism.
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stopping conditions.
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/1/title: How to choose an architecture and evaluate trade-offs
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Use few-shot prompting when a verbal description does not adequately convey the pattern; avoid using too many examples, which consume context and cause overfitting.
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach does more than work on the happy path. It also clarifies permissions, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare the options using the same criteria.
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Examples containing only easy cases can make the model confidently wrong on real inputs.
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to represent assumptions as explicit states or structured data, set clear limits, and retain enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “does not exist,” “not yet found,” and “cannot be checked.”
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not conceal errors with empty results.
claude-4-2-few-shot./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.

claude-4-3-structured-output./shortTitle: Structured Output Using Tool Use and JSON Schema
claude-4-3-structured-output./summary: The backend requires consistently typed invoice data and cannot parse prose responses. This lesson connects schemas, required fields, enums, and tool results into a verifiable decision.

claude-4-3-structured-output./sections/0/paragraphs/0: The schema is the starting point, required fields determine how the system transitions between states, and enums and tool results provide evidence for assessing whether the result is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem as this sequence instead of selecting a feature merely because its name is familiar.
claude-4-3-structured-output./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Define a strict schema, use enums and constraints, require the appropriate tool, and validate at the application boundary.
claude-4-3-structured-output./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before selecting a mechanism.
claude-4-3-structured-output./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and termination conditions.

claude-4-3-structured-output./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare all options using the same criteria.
claude-4-3-structured-output./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are observable and easy to roll back.

claude-4-3-structured-output./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to represent assumptions as states or structured data, set explicit limits, and retain enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “not present,” “not found yet,” and “cannot be verified.”
claude-4-3-structured-output./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not conceal errors with empty results.
claude-4-3-structured-output./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.

claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./objectiveTitle: Implement validation, retry, and feedback loops
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./summary: The extractor returns valid JSON, but the total does not match the line items. This lesson connects syntactic validation, semantic validation, targeted feedback, and a retry budget into a verifiable decision process.
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./opening/0: The extractor returns valid JSON, but the total does not match the line items.
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./opening/1: Do not learn objective 4.4 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present an almost-correct system and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/0/title: A Mental Model from Input to Outcome
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Syntactic validation is the starting point, semantic validation determines how the system transitions between states, and targeted feedback and a retry budget provide evidence for deciding whether the result is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem in this sequence instead of choosing a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Validate in layers, return the specific error and relevant values, limit retries, and then switch to a fallback or human review.
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before choosing a mechanism.
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stopping conditions.
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/1/title: Choosing an Architecture and Evaluating Trade-offs
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Retry when feedback can help correct the error; stop when the source is incomplete, requirements conflict, or the same error recurs.
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach does more than work on the happy path. It also clarifies permissions, context costs, side effects, the retry strategy, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare options using the same criteria.
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are observable and easy to roll back.
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Retrying the original prompt without adding a new signal often reproduces the same error and increases costs.
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to turn assumptions into explicit state or structured data, set clear limits, and preserve enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “not present,” “not yet found,” and “cannot be verified.”
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not conceal errors with empty results.
claude-4-4-validation-retry-feedback./sections/2/takeaways/1: Every retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.

claude-4-5-batch-processing./title: Batch processing: high throughput with full traceability
claude-4-5-batch-processing./summary: You need to classify 50,000 tickets overnight with clear cost and resume capabilities. This lesson connects custom_id, chunking, polling, and partial failure into a verifiable decision.
claude-4-5-batch-processing./opening/0: You need to classify 50,000 tickets overnight with clear cost and resume capabilities.
claude-4-5-batch-processing./opening/1: Do not learn section 4.5 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present an almost-correct system and ask you to select the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/0/title: A mental model from input to result
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/0/paragraphs/0: custom_id is the starting point, chunking determines how the system transitions between states, and polling and partial failure provide the evidence needed to determine whether the results are trustworthy. Always analyze the problem in this sequence instead of selecting a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Assign stable IDs, chunk within limits, save the submission state, poll with backoff, and reconcile each result by custom_id.
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before selecting a mechanism.
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stop conditions.
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/1/title: Selecting an architecture and evaluating trade-offs
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Use batch processing for workloads that do not require immediate responses; use realtime processing for interactions with a short latency SLO.
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach must work beyond the happy path. It must also clarify permissions, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare all options using the same criteria.
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Relying on response order or resubmitting the entire batch when only a few items fail causes duplicates and increases costs.
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to turn assumptions into explicit state or structured data, define clear limits, and retain enough provenance for the next step to distinguish among “absent,” “not yet found,” and “unable to verify.”
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not conceal errors with empty results.
claude-4-5-batch-processing./sections/2/takeaways/1: Every retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.

claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./objectiveTitle: Design multi-instance and multi-pass review architectures
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./summary: A critical report requires independent verification of claims, figures, and compliance. This lesson connects independent drafts, specialized critics, synthesis, and adjudication to produce a verifiable decision.
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./opening/0: A critical report requires independent verification of claims, figures, and compliance.
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./opening/1: Do not learn objective 4.6 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present a nearly correct system and ask you to select the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./sections/0/title: A mental model from input to outcome
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./sections/0/paragraphs/0: The independent draft is the starting point, the specialized critic determines how the system transitions between states, and synthesis and adjudication produce evidence for assessing whether the result is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem as this sequence instead of selecting a feature merely because its name is familiar.
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Separate the creator, criteria-specific reviewers, and synthesizer; keep their prompts and contexts sufficiently independent to avoid a shared blind spot.
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the initial state before selecting a mechanism.
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stopping conditions.

claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach must do more than work on the happy path. It must also clarify authority, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare all options using the same criteria.
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are observable and easy to reverse.
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Multiple agents given the same incorrect context may reach a highly confident consensus and still be wrong.
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to represent assumptions as state or structured data, set clear limits, and retain enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “does not exist,” “not yet found,” and “cannot be verified.”
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not conceal errors with empty results.
claude-4-6-multi-pass-review./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.

claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./title: Preserving Critical Context Across Long Conversations
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./shortTitle: Preserving Critical Context Across Long Conversations
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./summary: After many turns, the agent forgets a legal constraint established at the beginning of the session. This lesson connects working context, durable state, summary, and priority into a verifiable decision.

claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./opening/1: Do not study section 5.1 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present a nearly correct system and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/0/title: A Mental Model from Input to Outcome
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Working context is the starting point, durable state determines how the system transitions between states, and summary and priority provide evidence for determining whether the outcome is reliable. Always analyze the problem as this sequence instead of choosing a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Separate invariants, decisions, open issues, and evidence; update a structured summary instead of retaining the entire raw history.
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before choosing a mechanism.
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stopping conditions.
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/1/title: Choosing an Architecture and Evaluating Trade-offs
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Preserve the exact wording when it serves as evidence; summarize when only the state and decision are needed.
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good solution does more than work on the happy path. It also clarifies authority, context cost, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare options using the same criteria.
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Repeated summarization without source attribution can lose conditions, negations, or levels of certainty.
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to represent assumptions as state or structured data, set clear limits, and retain enough provenance for later steps to distinguish among “absent,” “not yet found,” and “cannot be verified.”
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not conceal errors with empty results.
claude-5-1-preserve-critical-context./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.

claude-5-2-escalation-ambiguity./objectiveTitle: Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns
claude-5-2-escalation-ambiguity./summary: The refund request does not specify an amount and may cause financial side effects. This lesson connects ambiguity class, risk threshold, clarifying question, and escalation path into a verifiable decision.

Always analyze the problem as this sequence instead of selecting a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-5-2-escalation-ambiguity./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Classify missing data, conflicts, and authorization issues; ask the smallest question that unblocks progress or hand off to a person with full context.

claude-5-2-escalation-ambiguity./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stopping conditions.
