claude-5-3-error-propagation./objectiveTitle: Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
claude-5-3-error-propagation./title: Propagating Errors Across Multi-Agent Systems Without Losing Meaning
claude-5-3-error-propagation./shortTitle: Propagating Errors Across Multi-Agent Systems Without Losing Meaning
claude-5-3-error-propagation./summary: A subagent cannot access a source, but the coordinator presents the result as verified. This lesson connects typed errors, causal chains, partial results, and fallbacks into a verifiable decision.
claude-5-3-error-propagation./opening/0: A subagent cannot access a source, but the coordinator presents the result as verified.
claude-5-3-error-propagation./opening/1: Do not study section 5.3 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present an almost-correct system and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery strategy.
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/0/title: A Mental Model from Input to Result
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/0/paragraphs/0: A typed error is the starting point, the causal chain determines how the system changes state, and partial results and fallbacks provide evidence for assessing whether the result is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem as this sequence instead of choosing a feature merely because its name is familiar.
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Each agent returns its status, error code, cause, available evidence, and retryability; the coordinator does not increase confidence when an upstream operation fails.
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before choosing a mechanism.
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have explicit success, failure, and stop conditions.
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/1/title: Choosing an Architecture and Evaluating Trade-offs
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Allow partial success when the consumer knows what is missing; fail closed if the missing portion affects safety or the fundamental correctness of the result.
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach does more than work on the happy path. It also clarifies permissions, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare all options using the same criteria.
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are observable and easy to roll back.
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Catching an exception and returning an empty array causes downstream components to misinterpret the result as “no data.”
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to represent assumptions as explicit states or structured data, set clear limits, and retain enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “none,” “not found yet,” and “unable to verify.”
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not conceal errors with empty results.
claude-5-3-error-propagation./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.

claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./title: Explore large codebases without overwhelming the context
claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./shortTitle: Explore large codebases without overwhelming the context
claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./summary: The monorepo contains millions of lines, but the bug involves only the auth callback and session store. This lesson connects search-first, the dependency path, progressive disclosure, and the working set into a verifiable decision.
claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./opening/0: The monorepo contains millions of lines, but the bug involves only the auth callback and session store.
claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./opening/1: Do not learn section 5.4 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present an almost-correct system and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./sections/0/title: A mental model from input to outcome
claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./sections/0/paragraphs/0: search-first is the starting point, the dependency path determines how the system transitions between states, and progressive disclosure and the working set provide evidence for determining whether the outcome is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem through this sequence instead of selecting a feature merely because its name is familiar.
claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Start with the symptom or symbol, follow callers and the data flow, record a small map, and expand it only when supported by evidence.
claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./sections/0/takeaways/0: Identify the input state before selecting a mechanism.
claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stop conditions.

claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach does more than work on the happy path. It also clarifies permissions, context cost, side effects, the retry strategy, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare all options using the same criteria.
claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Loading the entire repo causes the model to miss important details and spend context on unrelated files.
claude-5-4-large-codebase-context./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The remedy is to turn assumptions into explicit state or structured data, set clear limits, and retain enough provenance for later steps to distinguish among “absent,” “not found yet,” and “unable to verify.”

claude-5-5-human-review-confidence./objectiveTitle: Design human review workflows and confidence calibration
claude-5-5-human-review-confidence./summary: The classifier automatically handles clear-cut cases but requires human review for high-impact cases or weak evidence. This lesson connects risk tier, confidence evidence, review queue, and feedback capture into a verifiable decision process.
claude-5-5-human-review-confidence./opening/0: The classifier automatically handles clear-cut cases but requires human review for high-impact cases or weak evidence.
claude-5-5-human-review-confidence./opening/1: Do not study objective 5.5 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present an almost-correct system and ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-5-5-human-review-confidence./sections/0/title: A Mental Model from Input to Outcome
claude-5-5-human-review-confidence./sections/0/paragraphs/0: risk tier is the starting point, confidence evidence determines how the system transitions between states, and review queue and feedback capture provide the evidence needed to determine whether the outcome is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem in this sequence instead of selecting a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-5-5-human-review-confidence./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Combine confidence with impact, novelty, and data quality; provide the reviewer with the input, recommendation, evidence, and reasons for uncertainty.

claude-5-5-human-review-confidence./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.

claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./objectiveTitle: Preserve information provenance and handle uncertainty in synthesis
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./summary: Multiple documents conflict on the same metric, and the report must enable readers to verify the information themselves. This lesson connects source ID, claim-evidence links, conflict, and uncertainty language into a verifiable decision.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./opening/0: Multiple documents conflict on the same metric, and the report must enable readers to verify the information themselves.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./opening/1: Do not learn section 5.6 as an isolated definition. Architecture questions often present an approximately correct system and then ask you to choose the most appropriate boundary, control mechanism, or recovery approach.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/0/title: A Mental Model from Input to Outcome
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/0/paragraphs/0: source ID is the starting point, the claim-evidence link determines how the system transitions between states, and conflict and uncertainty language provide evidence for assessing whether the outcome is trustworthy. Always analyze the problem using this sequence instead of choosing a feature simply because its name is familiar.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/0/paragraphs/1: Associate each claim with its source, timestamp, and confidence level; preserve disagreements instead of forcing them into a single value.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/0/takeaways/0: Determine the input state before choosing a mechanism.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/0/takeaways/1: Every automated step must have success, failure, and stopping conditions.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/1/title: Choosing an Architecture and Evaluating Trade-offs
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Clearly distinguish inferences from facts; when evidence is missing, state that the conclusion is undetermined and explain how to verify it.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A good approach must do more than work on the happy path. It must also clarify permissions, context costs, side effects, retry behavior, and who is responsible when the evidence is insufficient.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/1/takeaways/0: Compare approaches using the same criteria.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/1/takeaways/1: Prefer boundaries that are easy to observe and reverse.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Combining content from multiple sources and then removing its provenance prevents readers from auditing the result or identifying a faulty source.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/2/paragraphs/1: The solution is to represent assumptions as explicit states or structured data, set clear limits, and preserve enough provenance for subsequent steps to distinguish among “does not exist,” “not yet found,” and “cannot be verified.”
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/2/takeaways/0: Do not conceal errors with empty results.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./sections/2/takeaways/1: A retry must have a reason, a new signal, and a budget.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./diagram/alt: Context Management & Reliability diagram for section 5.6
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./diagram/caption: Read from left to right: a request passes through the control boundary, action, and feedback loop before becoming an accepted outcome.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./visuals/0/caption: The synthesis table contains claim, source, observedAt, confidence, and conflict note fields.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./visuals/0/nodes/3/responsibility: Generate evidence and determine when to stop
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./decisionWalkthroughs/0/requirement: Multiple documents conflict on the same metric, and the report must enable readers to verify the information themselves.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/0: Do not expand permissions or data access beyond what is necessary.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/1: Errors, costs, and completion status must be observable.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./decisionWalkthroughs/0/constraints/2: The outcome must be independently verifiable.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/0/fit: Suitable when intermediate results determine the next step and a claim-evidence link is required.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/0/limita: Consumes more context and requires clear stopping conditions.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/name: Deterministic workflow with a validation gate
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/fit: Suitable when the sequence, permissions, and uncertainty language are known in advance.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./decisionWalkthroughs/0/options/1/limita: Less flexible when handling new situations that have not been modeled.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Clearly distinguish inferences from facts; when evidence is missing, state that the conclusion is undetermined and explain how to verify it.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Define the state and side effects.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Assess the risk if conflict handling fails.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Choose an approach that produces evidence at an acceptable cost.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./comparison/0/useWhen: The path depends on new observations and multiple valid branches exist.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./comparison/0/tradeOff: Requires a budget, guardrails, and better observability.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./comparison/1/useWhen: The sequence and state-transition conditions are clear, or compliance requirements apply.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./comparison/1/tradeOff: Less adaptable, but easier to audit and test.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./commonMistakes/0/wrong: A longer prompt alone can address the problem of combining content from multiple sources and then removing its provenance, which prevents readers from auditing the result or identifying a faulty source.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./commonMistakes/0/better: Encode important conditions in the schema, state, permissions, or validation instead of relying solely on wording.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./commonMistakes/0/reason: Architectural controls must exist at execution boundaries, not merely as intentions.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Measure success by whether the system produces a plausible-sounding answer.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./commonMistakes/1/better: Require evidence linked to uncertainty language and verify actual side effects.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./commonMistakes/1/reason: Fluency does not prove that the system completed the task correctly.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./examFocus/0: Recognize when source ID must be separated from the claim-evidence link.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./examFocus/1: Choose an approach that reduces the risk of this failure mode: combining content from multiple sources and then removing its provenance, which prevents readers from auditing the result or identifying a faulty source.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./examFocus/2: Explain the trade-off instead of merely naming a Claude feature.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./activity/context: Multiple documents conflict on the same metric, and the report must enable readers to verify the information themselves.

claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./activity/tasks/2: Design a failure path for this scenario: combining content from multiple sources and then removing its provenance prevents readers from auditing the result or identifying a faulty source.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Identify the minimum required data and permissions.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./activity/reasoningSteps/1: List success, retryable error, and terminal error states.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Link each conclusion to verifiable evidence.

claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: Associate each claim with its source, timestamp, and confidence level; preserve disagreements instead of forcing them into a single value.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: Acceptance criterion: uncertainty language must produce evidence, and the system must stop within its budget.
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: What changes if the conflict information is unreliable?
claude-5-6-provenance-uncertainty./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: Which metrics would you use to measure quality and cost?
